input.background: The USGA wants to compare the mean distances of four different brands of golf balls when struck with a driver. A completely randomized design is used, where Iron Byron, the USGA's robotic golfer, hits a random sample of 10 balls from each brand in a random sequence. The distance for each hit is recorded, and the results are organized by brand in Table 9.3.
input.question: Set up the test to compare the mean distances for the four brands. Use $\alpha=.10$.

output.answer:
To compare the mean distances of the four brands, we first specify the hypotheses to be tested. Denoting the population mean of the $i$th brand by $\mu_i$, we test:

$$
H_0: \mu_1=\mu_2=\mu_3=\mu_4
$$

$$
H_a: \text{The mean distances differ for at least two of the brands.}
$$

The test statistic compares the variation among the four treatment (Brand) means to the sampling variability within each of the treatments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Test statistic: } F=\frac{\mathrm{MST}}{\mathrm{MSE}} \\
& \text{Rejection region: } F>F_\alpha=F_{10} \\
& \text{with } v_1=(k-1)=3 \mathrm{df} \text{ and } v_2=(n-k)=36 \mathrm{df}
\end{aligned}
$$

From Table V in Appendix D: Tables, we find $F_{10} \approx 2.25$ for 3 and 36 df. Thus, we will reject $H_0$ if $F>2.25$. (See Figure 9.11.)

The assumptions necessary to ensure the validity of the test are as follows:
1. The samples of 10 golf balls for each brand are selected randomly and independently.
2. The probability distributions of the distances for each brand are normal.
3. The variances of the distance probability distributions for each brand are equal.
